text:
Romanian swimmer David Popovici clinched the gold medal in the 200m freestyle at the Paris Olympics on July 29, 2024, delivering a race with a dramatic finish.

Popovici started strong, securing second place after the first length of the pool. By the 100-meter mark, he was third, but he surged ahead in the final 50 meters, overtaking British swimmer Matthew Richards in the closing moments of the race.

The 19-year-old Popovici, already a double world champion and four-time European champion, secured Romania’s first-ever Olympic title in men’s swimming with a time of 1 minute 44.72 seconds, edging out Richards by just two-hundredths of a second (1 minute 44.74 seconds). Popovici demonstrated his prowess by posting the fastest times in both the heats (1 minute 45.65 seconds) and the semi-finals (1 minute 44.53 seconds).



This victory marks a significant achievement for Popovici, who finished fourth in the 200m freestyle final at the Tokyo Olympics three years ago.Read more aboutPorsche Romania appoints David Popovici as Friend of the Brandread more

Popovici, who has earned Romania’s first medal at the 2024 Olympic Games, is also set to compete in the 100m freestyle in Paris, with heats scheduled for Tuesday at 12:15 p.m. and the semifinals at 9:30 p.m.

Romanian swimming’s last Olympic medals were won in 2004, when Camelia Potec claimed gold in the 200m freestyle and Razvan Florea took bronze in the 200m backstroke in Athens.

Current status for Romania at the Olympics 2024

Romania has qualified for the women’s team final in artistic gymnastics at the Paris Olympic Games, marking their first participation in 12 years. The last time Romania reached an Olympic final was in 2012 in London, where they won the bronze medal in the team competition. The Romanian team secured the final qualifying position with a total of 159.497 points after competing in the first subdivision.

The USA team, led by Simone Biles, dominated the qualifiers with a total of 172.296 points. The other teams advancing to the finals are Italy (166.861 points), China (166.28), Brazil (166.499), Japan (162.196), Canada (161.563), and Great Britain (160.830).

Simone Biles was the standout gymnast in the all-around competition, scoring a total of 59.566 points. Romania’s Ana Maria Barbosu also qualified for the all-around final, ranking 16th. Despite notable performances, some top gymnasts like Chiles (USA) and Iorio (Italy) did not qualify due to their teams already having two athletes with higher scores.

Romanian gymnast Sabrina Maneca Voinea qualified for both the beam and floor finals, while Ana Maria Barbosu also made it to the floor final. Voinea scored 14.000 on the beam, ranking 5th among the qualifiers, and Barbosu ranked 17th with 13.533 points. Amalia Ghigoarta and Lilia Cosman scored 13.266 and 12.833 points respectively, ranking 30th and 41st.

In the floor exercise, Voinea scored 13.800, placing 4th, and Barbosu ranked 8th with a score of 13.600. Ghigoarta and Cosman scored 13.333 and 12.466 points, ranking 16th and 53rd respectively.

Barbosu also ranked 15th in vault with a score of 13.633, while Ghigoarta placed 43rd in parallel bars with a score of 13.066.

Photos courtesy of COSR
